my_thoughts: 'Once'—they admit it. One mistake. Now teacher asks: how many times reminded yourself? This sets up the devastating comparison. If they say 'hundreds' (which they will), the ratio speaks: 1 action vs 100 punishments. That's not learning, that's torture. The quantification makes excess visible. Can't say 'thinking about mistakes' when ratio is 1:100. Hypothesis: they'll say hundreds, teacher will name it 'self-torture,' and the distinction will land.
accumulated_insight: T7: Student quantifies error: 'Once'—admits mistake happened one time. Teacher sets up comparison: 'And how many times did you remind yourself of it and call yourself stupid?' This creates quantitative contrast: 1 mistake vs X reminders. The comparison will expose disproportionate response. Predicting surface_emotion because seeing ratio produces recognition of excess.
visible_response: And how many times did you remind yourself of it and call yourself stupid?
